data.title: Intuit Is A Fintech Fortress, But You're Paying For It Already
data.description:
Intuit Overview

Intuit (NASDAQ:INTU), best known for its tax return software, TurboTax, provides varying financial management services (e.g., Credit Karma, QuickBooks). The company is a highly successful Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) that generates billions each year in free cash flow (FCF), with FCF margins exceeding 30%. However, its stock in the past year has significantly underperformed industry benchmarks, returning -8% versus SPDR S&P 500 (SPY) gains of 6%.

Intuit's underperformance is despite consistent beats on revenue and EPS in 2024. So, what is happening?

The following article reveals that Intuit’s premium valuation, while justified, is making it difficult for the SaaS giant to outperform its required rate of return.

Earnings

On the income statement, Intuit divides its revenues into two categories: “product and other” and “services.” The former includes revenue from selling software licenses like Quickbooks. The latter regards income from ongoing services and subscriptions, including cloud-based offerings like TurboTax Live. In the three months ended January 31, 2025 (its fiscal Q2), service and product revenues were $3.249 billion and $714 million, respectively. The total represented 17% year-over-year revenue growth. Notably, its revenue mix leaned heavier on its service segment (82% versus 79.5% during the same period last year). Cost of service and product revenue was $880 million and $20 million, respectively. This makes sense. Licensing existing software involves very little cost. Providing services like “expert tax advice” is more costly. So, Intuit’s increasing reliance on services is probably impacting its overall profitability. This is unavoidable, though. Its software can only be licensed so many times, but services are perpetual. After accounting for S&M ($1.204 billion), R&D ($716 million), and G&A ($389 million) expenses, Intuit’s operating income in FQ2 was $593 million. Net income was $471 million. Looking at cash flows, Intuit’s FCF margin in FQ2 was 26%. In the trailing twelve months, its FCF margin was ~33%. Obviously, this can vary quarter to quarter, which is why I also present the annual figure.

Intuit reiterated its full fiscal year 2025 guidance. Revenue is expected to be between $18.16 billion and $18.347 billion, representing growth of 12%-13%.

Recent Developments

Intuit competes with many companies. However, anyone who's done taxes through a software like TurboTax knows how “sticky” it can be. No one wants to reference years of documents during tax season when a software keeps track of all that information for you. This makes it inconvenient to “switch.” This is great for Intuit because they are the market leader. So while the market becomes more saturated and competitive, Intuit has a good amount of “staying power.”

Last year, Intuit underwent restructuring to align with its AI-driven initiatives. This included laying off “1,800” employees, which represented 10% of its total workforce. Its intention was to hire employees with AI experience back. This resulted in a restructuring charge of $223 million in FQ4.

Intuit’s voyage into AI-assist solutions is best represented by its Intuit Assist, which utilizes generative AI to deliver personalized recommendations across products like TurboTax and QuickBooks. In addition, Intuit’s “agentic AI initiatives” streamline the onboarding process for new customers and automates processing of invoices. The careful and successful integration of AI into these products are so important for Intuit’s products moving forward. A lot of work in financial services is quite tedious and Intuit’s AI features could really help users become more efficient. Intuit’s robust cash generation and leadership in the financial services market really give it an edge here. It has the resources available to accelerate the development of these important features and remain differentiated from its competitors.

Financial Health

As of January 31, Intuit had $2.435 billion in cash and cash equivalents. Total current assets were $9.135 billion, while total current liabilities were $7.179 billion. Subsequently, Intuit’s current ratio is over 1, which signals solid liquidity. Intuit does owe $5.76 billion in long-term debt. Given Intuit’s robust cash generation, its debt appears manageable.

Valuation Assumptions

There are some caveats to consider when modeling Intuit in a discounted cash flow (DCF) model. Its fiscal period differs from calendar periods. So, my Year 1 input is an estimate for Intuit’s fiscal period ending July 2025. To account for this, I adjusted “Fraction of Year Elapsed” to 0.5 so that I do not double count cash flows (Intuit’s cash & equivalents are as of January 31). 14% year-over-year revenue growth and 32% FCF margins are a blend of historical performance and future expectations. Everything else is pretty standard.

So, on a pure cash flow basis, my model suggests Intuit is 9% overvalued.

My sensitivity analysis, which tells us how adjustments to key inputs (revenue growth and FCF margin) affect valuation, suggests that Intuit is priced for ~15.5% revenue growth or ~35% FCF margins.

Next, I ran a Monte Carlo simulation based on the inputs from my DCF model. The issue with my DCF model is that it presents only one scenario based on specific parameters. My Monte Carlo simulation accounts for variability in inputs like growth, margins, and beta

Intuit’s price still falls above the median of $556.61 per share.

Outside of "intrinsic value," another problem with Intuit is that its multiples also suggest the stock is valued at a premium. In the past few years, Intuit’s P/E ratio was as high as 90+. It has gradually compressed over time, but it is still high relative to its sector.

Admittedly, stocks are more complicated than their intrinsic value based on projected cash flows. They are often also priced by the market relative to peers and according to multiples like P/E and EV/EBITDA.

Over time, Intuit’s multiples are expected to contract more. This makes it challenging for the stock to outperform its required rate of return (9.88%) even when the company is delivering strong 14% revenue growth and 32% FCF margins.

INTU Stock: Investment Recommendation

In conclusion, Intuit’s established leadership, strong brand, and proactive AI investments should keep it on top of the competitive financial technology market. However, its stock appears slightly overvalued on a cash flow basis, and gradual multiple compression is making it difficult for INTU to outperform. This may continue for some time. However, Intuit could offer compelling upside in the reasonable event AI integrations help boost its profitability or growth outlook. This merits a “Hold” (market perform) recommendation. If Intuit can demonstrate operational improvements to boost cash generation or if its stock falls near Monte Carlo’s median value of $556.61, it should warrant a “Buy.” Until then, it’s challenging to envision INTU outperforming the market. Some risks to investment include continued stock underperformance despite fundamental strength, competition from other financial technology companies, regulatory changes, and macroeconomic headwinds.

data.title: Voya Intermediate Bond Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Total return approach, investing across full spectrum of the fixed income market including up to 20% in below investment grade securities.

Portfolio review

Strong economic data continued to come through in the final quarter of 2024, which had the impact of driving spreads tighter while reversing the rate rally experienced in 3Q24. The gross domestic product report for 3Q24 once again came in elevated (initially reporting at 2.8% and later revised to 3.1%) driven largely by strong consumer and government spending, which grew at 3.7% and 5.1%, respectively. Notably, consumer spending accounted for roughly 80% of the overall growth, highlighting its critical role in the economy.

Inflationary pressures remained a key concern. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) surprised to the upside in early October, and while subsequent data showed stability in both CPI and Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation, both remained elevated. Core PCE, the US Federal Reserve's preferred measure of inflation, finished the quarter at 2.8% year-over-year, slightly above where it was trending in the summer. Notably, core goods prices, which had experienced a deflationary trend for several months, began to reaccelerate.

Labor market dynamics continued to show signs of softening without entering a state of deterioration. Job gains remained reasonable, and although the unemployment rate resumed its upward trajectory, it remained below the high watermark set earlier in the year. Meanwhile, wage growth remained strong, which helps explains both the resilience of consumer spending and the stickiness of services inflation.

While the Fed continued to deliver rate cuts at both the November and December meetings, the December cut was accompanied by more "hawkish" elements. Specifically, the Fed's Dot plot indicated only two cuts projected for 2025 down from four in the previous iteration. Additionally, officials moved their projection for both growth and inflation higher, while their projection for the unemployment rate moved lower and only slightly above the current level.

Overall, the fourth quarter of 2024 was characterized by resilient labor market dynamics, strong economic growth and sticky inflation. The impact on fixed income performance was mixed, with yields rising and credit spreads tightening, leading to modestly negative total returns for most fixed income sectors.

For the quarter, the Fund outperformed the Index on NAV basis. Sector allocation and security selection decisions contributed, while duration and yield curve positions had minimal impact. Our overweight to credit and underweight in Treasuries broadly contributed. Our overweight to asset-backed securities (ABS) was the largest individual contributor, as the sector continued to tighten in sympathy with other high quality spread sectors. Our overweight to commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) was a close second, as fundamental factors improved, and the sector continued to benefit from an active primary market. Additionally, positive security selection results in CMBS were realized due to our bias towards higher yielding tranches. Security selection decisions with agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) also contributed, due to our off-benchmark collateralized mortgage obligation (CMO) holdings which outperformed despite underperformance of Index constituents. Finally, due to our neutral duration position, the move higher in rates had limited impact on relative performance.

Current strategy and outlook

Looking forward, we continue to expect economic growth will remain resilient. The recent election outcome further strengthens this view due to election optimism and productivity gains from deregulation. Meanwhile, we expect inflation to resume its downward trend, driven by moderating service prices. In this area however, the election result lowers our conviction as an adverse policy mix of deficit spending, tariffs and stricter immigration limits would sustain higher levels of demand while constraining supply. That said, we believe a negative market reaction would likely compel the administration to temper those policies before they derail growth.

Assuming this is the case, and inflation does not reaccelerate, we expect the Fed will tolerate inflation that is slightly above their target and deliver a few more rate cuts in an effort to preserve the current cycle.

Overall, this backdrop should be favorable for credit sectors, however we continue to favor high-quality, shorter-dated assets given where valuations are. Over the quarter we reduced our allocation to longer dated investment grade ((IG)) corporates and are now positioned with an underweight from a spread duration perspective. Similarly, we maintain only a modest allocation to high yield corporates, while high quality securitized makes up much of our active risk. If there is a misstep in policy implementation, we believe we are well positioned to capitalize on the short-lived market dislocation and will look to add market risk further out the curve.

data.title: Protean Funds Scandinavia AB March 2025 Partner Letter
data.description:
Dear Partners,

Today marks the first day of trading for our new fund Protean Aktiesparfond Norden. It is a Nordic long-only fund that combines the low fees of index funds with the potential of active management to outperform the index. The best of two worlds. In addition, the fee will be lowered as the fund grows bigger, sharing the scale advantages with investors. We launch the fund with approximately USD 30m in AuM, and we thank all who have dared to believe in the concept from day one. Now we will try to prove ourselves.

Local indices had a terrible March. While managing to (marginally) beat its benchmark, Protean Small Cap suffered also. It returned -6.1%, outperforming its index by 0.1%. Since launching in June 2023, it has gained 37.1%, which is 23% ahead of the Carnegie Nordic Small Cap Index.

The hedge fund Protean Select returned -1.2% in March, staying true to its objective of avoiding large drawdowns. We are approaching the 3-year mark in April, and since launch the fund has returned 27.6% with (far) less than a third of the market’s volatility.

This month’s letter elaborates on why on earth we are starting a low-cost active fund. We follow up last month’s “Is this really happening?” with the conclusions from spending time on the ground in both the US and Mexico (hint: it’s not great). And, as usual, provide color on winners and losers in both the hedge fund and the small cap fund.

Thank you for being an investor!

// Team Protean

Protean Small Cap

- Carl’s update for March

Protean Small Cap returned -6.1% in March. That is 0.1%-points ahead of the CSXRN (SEK) benchmark index for the month. This puts the fund 23.0%-points ahead of our index (CSRXN SEK) since inception. The fund now manages ca. SEK530m. Thank you for your trust.

Rejlers, Lindex and Getinge (OTCPK:GNGBF) were our main winners for the month. Rejlers released new long-term targets during the month, reinforcing our view that this technical consultant has potential to grow both revenues and margins in the coming years. Valuation is far below its peers, despite a solid track record in recent years.

We were also helped by those with pure Nordic exposure, with names such as Coor, Ambea and Meko performing relatively well during the month. The well-executed IPO of Asker also netted us a good benefit.

Detractors were plentiful during a very poor month for Nordic equities. Devyser was our biggest loser during the month, with MT Højgaard and Sdiptech (OTC:SDTHF) close behind.

The big movements during March also created opportunities and we have added a few new names to the portfolio. The Swedish medtech Arjo (OTCPK:ARRJF) offers a compelling risk/reward, with the severe flu season acting as a benefit in the very near-term. We have long been skeptical of Arjo, as inflated expectations were attached to the SEM scanner product which proved emblematic for a period of overpromising and underdelivering for Arjo. As this costly endeavor now has been scrapped, as well as the CEO being replaced, we believe this rebasing has created a good entry opportunity. Arjo’s bed rental business should have helped them during the first quarter.

The Finnish landlord Kojamo (OTCPK:KOJAF) also entered the portfolio during March. Having added a lot of new capacity to the market during the last couple of years, vacancy rates increased markedly for Kojamo during 2023-2024. We believe vacancy rates will improve in 2025, as the new capacity is gradually being absorbed by the continued urbanization in Finland as well as very limited new construction taking place currently. The share traded at all-time low levels during March, despite this being a ‘safe haven’ (although we often doubt such things exist) in a period of uncertainty in the global arena. We have also gradually increased our holdings in several names, these including long-term attractive growth stories such as Sdiptech and Vitrolife (OTCPK:VTRLY).

Finding new names also means selling others. BTS had to leave but will remain on our radar. While their leadership training session might add a lot of long-term value to its clients, they are also a discretionary spend that can be delayed. We have sold Elekta (OTCPK:EKTAF) and OMA Savings Bank. While trading at intriguing levels, these names both suffer from a level of complexity and lack of near-term operational momentum that makes them unattractive now.

The ten largest positions in Protean Small Cap as we enter April:

Protean Select

– Pontus’ update for March

*We illustrate our performance by showing a comparison with the NHX Equities index. This is an index constructed from the performance of 54 Nordic hedge funds focusing on equity strategies. NHX is published after our Partner Letter, so updates with one-month lag in the chart above. We aim to have positive returns regardless of the market, but no return is created in a vacuum, and a net-long strategy will correlate. Our hurdle rate is 6.3275% annualized (4% + 90-day Swedish T-bills). All figures are net of fees and ratios in the above charts are calculated using monthly returns.

Protean Select returned -1.2% in a murky March where local indices were down around 7-8%. While it’s not pleasing to have a small drawdown, this is an acceptable result for a net long strategy. We averaged 25% net exposure for the month. Since inception in May 2022 the fund has generated performance of 27.6%, with low volatility.

Top contributors were all short positions: index futures, a basket of small caps, Atlas Copco (OTCPK:ATLKY), Lagercrantz. Notable detractors were Novo Nordisk (NVO), Devyser, Hexagon (OTCPK:HXGBF) and RaySearch (OTCPK:RSLBF).

We exit March with 23% beta-adjusted net long exposure and 119% gross exposure (adjusted for cash and cash equivalents). The portfolio remains diversified, with no single position >4% of assets.

To survive

Things that have never happened, happen all the time. I think about this whenever someone draws historical parallels about markets, levels, valuations and other ephemeral things. There is a first for everything.

I think managing a hedge fund is about generating decent returns, but it is also about protecting capital. Year to date the fund is up 1.6%. Tolerable.

Protean Aktiesparfond Norden

– Richard’s update

The fund has now launched. While this is all very exciting, we must remember investing is a game of patience. Compounding capital with a sensible and low-cost approach should be boring.

Nowhere is patience more rewarded than in the stock market. Patience, low costs and avoiding silly and overvalued companies that do not create value is how one creates the best conditions for potentially beating the index.

This is what we are trying to do, and the more that join, the lower the cost will be. That’s the beauty of Aktiesparfonden.

Going forward we will intermittently publish thoughts, updates and observations on www.aktiesparfonden.se .

Pontus’ personal reflection

Today—April 1st—is the first trading day for Protean Nordic Equity Savings Fund, or Aktiesparfonden in Swedish. From the outside, it might seem like this marks the beginning, but for me, it’s more of a part closure.

For many years, I've been fascinated by the rise of index funds. It's entirely logical from an investor's perspective: when active funds consistently underperform indices over time, we are satisfied simply getting the market return. Countless research papers draw the same conclusion: the primary reason active funds underperform the index is their higher fees.

The reverse is also true: both Charles Schwab and Jack Bogle—the fathers of low-cost financial products—say that the greatest innovation index funds brought to the market is precisely that: low fees. For those interested in the emergence of this massive industry, I recommend Charles Schwab's autobiography "Invested" and "Trillions" by journalist Robin Wigglesworth.

Beat the index, lower the fees, or go away

So why don't active funds lower their fees to compete with the inflow into index funds? Because they can't, and because they don't have to.

Many find investing complicated, and stocks especially difficult. Complexity earns respect from the public for financial products, prompting people to listen to authorities. It is often difficult to understand exactly what you're paying for a fund, and comparing funds presents additional pitfalls.

The investment market is sluggish. Many are satisfied simply listening to the bank’s salesperson and the funds offered. People accept both the (poor) returns and the ('high') fees because they have better, more important things to focus on, which is entirely reasonable.

The occupational pension market, however, is worse. Pension insurances are procured from a handful of oligopolists who, although it has improved, charge unclear "capital fees" and take at least half of any management fees from funds that manage to qualify for their, often-limited, selections. Encouraged by private equity-owned advisory firms, a structure is cemented where funds must be expensive (especially compared to index funds) for the equation with thousands of advisors and nationwide offices to be profitable. Fund selections are not driven by what's best for the customer. A cheap fund faces a double hurdle to be included in pension solutions. Consumer power is sidelined.

If I had to guess why a certain index fund is Sweden's largest, I'd say it's probably because it's also Sweden's most expensive. And ESG labelled. Double bingo: everyone scratches their bureaucratic itch without offending anyone.

Big gets bigger

Established fund companies are living on borrowed time with their expensive funds. Outflows and underperformance accelerate consolidation— driven by a panic to continue harvesting the economies of scale that large funds provide. It doesn't require more managers, administrators, or bigger offices to manage 50 billion compared to 50 million. Costs are essentially fixed from day one. After a few billion, every additional managed krona is pure profit. Lower fees? Forget it.

Consolidation is a dangerous strategy: research shows that size negatively correlates with returns. Larger funds find it (even) harder to beat the index. If you compare Sweden’s 5-6 largest funds, they predominantly hold similar top 10 investments. Their size forces them to crows in large companies. Small-cap funds face a similar issue. It's hard to significantly deviate from the index with a large fund. It requires uncomfortable decisions. Employees, being salaried, have limited incentives to deviate substantially from the index (which – remember – is required to beat it). It's better to be wrong in a group and not stand out. I believe we need more owner-led fund companies, not fewer.

The market slowly moves towards banning commissions. It's unreasonable for distributors to retain 50% of fund management fees. Increasingly more platforms (we cheer the upcoming launch of challenger Montrose!) and independent advisors return all fund commissions/kickbacks to customers.

Our hedge fund Protean Select and small-cap fund Protean Small Cap stand out because they are small and highly active. They aren't typical funds; we've tried to optimize for returns, not to maximize assets under management and thus income for the fund company. That’s why they trade monthly and have a size cap. They cannot—and should not—compete with large and index funds. But someone should.

Why Equity Savings Fund?

Years ago, I had a complex idea: let management fees compound into new fund units, with returns benefiting investors—allowing them to enjoy compound interest effects usually lost when fees are deducted annually. The problem was fund companies wouldn't have income until investors withdrew funds, which could take a very long time (think pension savings). Another issue was bookkeeping and taxation. It was (still is?) unfeasible.

For Protean Funds, founded three years ago and recently turning profitable with two niche products, starting a fund designed to challenge industry fees isn't obvious. To be profitable with low fees you need a large fund —several billion at least. So, what to do?

Reading William Green’s excellent book "Richer, Wiser, Happier" sparked an idea. It described Nomad Partnership's focus on companies whose products improved and became cheaper with growth. Economies of scale were shared with customers, creating a self-reinforcing cycle. Amazon (AMZN) and Costco (COST) were prime examples. Could this be done in fund management?

There’s a strategy likely to outperform indices long-term even at substantial sizes: focusing on proven, value-creating businesses, avoiding costly mistakes—like large investment firms such as Investor or Berkshire Hathaway. Low costs, long horizons, solid companies.

The uniqueness of sharing

This insight isn't unique. Several successful funds follow long-term, quality-focused strategies despite high fees. Examples include Sweden’s Selektiv funds from Handelsbanken, some Carnegie funds, or Fundsmith in the UK, which grew to £245 billion under management. Terry Smith’s strategy: "Long-term, reasonable companies, minimal activity." Wise man, Terry.

But—what they DON'T do is lower fees as the fund grows. THAT shares economies of scale. THAT compensates for large funds' difficulty in outperforming indices. THAT lowers the barrier to competing with index funds. It also reduces profitability, which is why no one does it.

Pitching this Equity Savings Fund to an investor in a fund management company would be laughable: actively managed, Nordic, long-only, needs billions to be profitable.

“Are you nuts, you’re NEVER going to make money from that fund. People want index funds or sexy thematic funds.”

Yet, here we are

On the day of launch. With a couple hundred million from anchor investors believing it should exist and could succeed.

My dream: lower the fees at 10 billion, again at 20, 30, and once more at 50 billion. I believe someone needs to challenge passive investing. It is vital for maintaining the Nordic ownership model which has served us well and ensuring effective market pricing and functioning. Without a thriving eco-system of independent fund managers, how do you suppose IPO’s of smaller companies will be done in the future?

I hope investors in Aktiesparfonden/The Equity Savings Fund have patience. We will evaluate performance after three years, seriously after five. It will underperform the index at times—this is nearly certain. Sometimes the underperformance could last for a long time. But if we're more right than wrong, it'll turn out well over time.

Off we go.

Is This Really Happening?

Part II – Remain vigilant

A month ago, I wrote “I’m mildly surprised to see stock markets taking this seismic geopolitical shift in stride and see few reasons to position ourselves other than cautiously.”

Since then, our local markets have tumbled 6-7% during, and the wave of relentless nonsense from the US shows no sign of abating. During March I physically went to the US and Mexico to get first-hand information, and to cut out the middlemen doing the interpretations for us in the media and research departments. I met with heads of business, consultants, Economists, senior diplomats and journalists.

I came back strategically more concerned than when I left, and during the trip added short positions in several USD-sensitive and US-exposed Nordic companies.

Thoughts on global politics

Just as the stock market, diplomacy, and business worlds now oscillate between two opposing thoughts: "it's just a negotiation tactic, it will pass," and "damn, these are entirely new rules now—might makes right—we must act swiftly."

The senior diplomats I spoke with admit it increasingly appears as a historical shift rather than an anomaly, and it's probably correct to have both a bit of Weltschmerz and prepare for "the worst" (unclear exactly what).

Perhaps it’s as simple as someone who believes they've received a mission from God finding it easier to justify the collateral damage that results when uncomfortable or unpopular decisions are implemented. Countering this is the pettiness revealed in various crypto shenanigans and tit-for-tat models involving payments for meetings and favours. It doesn’t quite fit the narrative to worry about Trump being driven by uncompromising patrimonialism or Bannon’s deglobalization frenzy, when he’s simultaneously cashing in relatively small sums through near-outright fraud.

Tactical Discussion

"Resistance" to Trump has so far been disorganized, fragmented, and lacking direction and management. As the shock wears off (about now?), reactions from both business (to be seen, excluding Tesla’s weak warning?) and civil society/Democrats (the ongoing mutiny against Schumer is telling) will emerge.

Less media-friendly, one executive order after another is being struck down by judges in various jurisdictions. There's a good chance the narrative will stabilize, if not reverse, in the near term.

The problem is this reasoning might be short-lived. Playing both demand and supply-side economics simultaneously means demand hits immediately while supply takes a long time, structurally characterized by "moving more manufacturing to the U.S. through tariff threats, transactional security policy, and a weakened dollar." Anecdotally, house prices in Washington are down 20% in one month due to mass layoffs at various institutions. Some things move incredibly fast.

The U.S. Midterms aren't until November 2026. The entire House and 33 of 100 Senate seats are up for election. It’s suggested this election could moderate Trump’s rhetoric to avoid scaring off voters in tight races. When does this campaign start? Early 2026? Still too far away for tactical considerations, but it’s a factor.

The European picture differs. Suddenly, its largest country is abandoning a debt brake in place since Weimar (anyone remembering hyperinflation is long gone). European sovereign yields have seen unprecedented movements recently—a straightforward supply/demand situation.

Approaching this cynically, within six months a (temporary?) "solution" to the Ukraine war may emerge. Trump needs a win. Ukraine has signalled its willingness (how could they not?). Russia is inscrutable, but endlessly grinding down its young men (and some tens of thousands of North Korean p*rn-surfers) for negligible territorial gains seems unsustainable. If all involved, including an EU viewing a ceasefire positively (despite lost Ukrainian territory), can save face, a truce will ultimately happen.

A revitalized European collaboration can bring positives. I'm pleasantly surprised by the Omnibus package cutting some reporting red tape for European businesses—the first-ever EU regulation reduction? More of that, please. Continue reforming the CFRD directive and eliminate virtue-signalling "Article 8" funds.

First-level thinking perhaps, but further debt-financed European expansion will drive growth and demand. Unemployment, the critical economic variable, will not be our issue when building defence infrastructure, stockpiling deterrence materials, and hiring many young men as soldiers and officers.

What we will suffer from eventually is that defence investment is unproductive, akin to funding dividends with increased debt. There are counterarguments—military R&D has historically produced disproportionate global productivity-enhancing innovations. Perhaps superconductors or fusion power will finally be solved? Weak arguments all around, though, as they cut both ways, and there is no progress or confidence when security issues make us fear for our collective future.

Tactical conclusion: One might speculate that expectations have soon adjusted, which would likely provide a short-lived tactical bottom.

Strategic Discussion

My stomach churns further (beyond the already existing discomfort) knowing senior diplomats share my bigger picture concerns. The reshaping—or dismantling—of the Western rules-bases world order is alarmingly swift. Speculations about "the Mar-a-Lago Accord" provoke disbelief and distress. Transactional global "leadership" akin to Jackson Pollock's chaos.

The intersection I watch closely is between blatant corruption (somewhat tolerable—"he's an idiot") and ideology. The US administration’s willingness to accept stock market declines or even recession indicates Trump 2.0 is far more ideologically driven than Trump 1.0. Equally troubling are appointments clearly unqualified professionally but highly qualified for loyalty.

Orwell couldn’t have scripted Trump better, yet his henchmen nod fervently. Witnessing a cult emerge in real-time is fascinating. Navarro, a Cuban anti-communist, flattering Putin causes considerable cognitive dissonance (though Putin isn’t necessarily communist, rather an autocratic traditionalist). Seeing 90% Trump propaganda merchandise at the stands at Dulles airport feels disturbingly North Korean.

Yet, one should remain aware of psychological proximity bias clouding judgment. Sensational headlines and dystopian predictions (Atwood or Huxley like scenarios) are tempting during uncertainty. Remember, we are likely experiencing shock-and-awe from the new administration. Competing in pessimism feels safer now. Who wants to be branded a Pollyanna?

Reflexively (to borrow from Soros), Europe enhancing defence capabilities reduces U.S. influence proportionately. This fact hasn't widely registered yet, but it remains true. Germany, a dormant juggernaut, might replace China as its primary market with European rearmament. Rheinmetall potentially converting VW factories into tank production is a telling sign. Interesting years ahead.

China holds roughly $750 billion in U.S. Treasuries; a coordinated EU27 holds quadruple that. Who has leverage?

A road leading directly into Dante’s seventh circle cannot be discounted. Markets haven't priced that in.

If China moves on Taiwan, if the U.S. escalates rhetoric against Greenland (as it undoubtedly is), Panama, or Canada, if Turkey pivots towards Russia, or another Middle Eastern conflict (Iran holding military drills with Russia and China, smuggling oil continuously, seems a prime target) ignites, normal distribution assumptions in financial markets collapse, and become dominated entirely by left tail risks.

Strategic conclusion: Remain vigilant. Capital preservation should be prioritized. A scenario where not just the world order but also global markets collapse is non-zero in probability.

A telling sign of a bigger shift in the making is that for the first time in as long as I can remember, the USD is not rallying when other safe haven assets are. With lower consumer confidence and the introduction of tariffs, comes both a negative growth impulse and an inflationary driver.

Take notice: the market is telling us there is stagflation ahead.

The monthy reminder

We optimize for performance, not for convenience, size, or marketing. You can withdraw money only quarterly (monthly in Small Cap). We tell you very little about our holdings. Our strategy is tricky to describe as we aim to be versatile. A hedge fund can lose money even if markets are up. We charge a performance fee if we do well. You do not get a discount if you have a larger sum to invest. We do not have a long track record.

Aktiesparfonden’s reminder

We aim to generate above index returns over 3-5 years, but there are no guarantees. The fund is daily traded, but that doesn’t mean you should. To beat the index, you need to deviate from the index. This means taking uncomfortable positions. Be aware that the fund can underperform the index during periods. Sometimes, long periods. We lower the fee as the fund grows. The first 10 basis point cut comes at 10bn SEK in AuM.

Thank you for being an investor.

Pontus Dackmo

CEO & Investment Manager

Protean Funds Scandinavia AB

DISCLAIMER: Investments in a fund can both increase and decrease in value. You are not guaranteed preservation of invested capital.

data.title: Colony Bankcorp: An Attractively Priced Georgia-Focused Regional Bank
data.description:
Introduction

Colony Bankcorp (NYSE:CBAN) is the holding company of the Colony Bank, a Georgia-based financial institution and the largest bank in the state that isn’t headquartered in Atlanta.

At the end of 2024, the bank had 34 locations in Georgia, one in Alabama and one in Florida.

A look back at the 2025 performance

In FY 2024, Colony Bankcorp reported a total interest income of $137.2M, an increase of almost 10% compared to the $125M in 2023. It goes without saying the interest expenses increased as well, and this resulted in a net interest income of $76.1M, a decrease of approximately $2.1M compared to the FY 2023 net interest income.

As the income statement above also shows, the total non-interest income increased by approximately 10% to $39.4M as the higher gain on the sale of SBA loans offset the negative impact of $1.8M related to the sale of securities. That being said, the bank’s total non-interest expenses decreased as well, and this resulted in a total pre-tax and pre-provision income of $32.6M, compared to $30.8M in FY 2023.

After deducting the $5.7M in income taxes, the net income was $23.9M, representing an EPS of $1.36 per share. The bank recently hiked its quarterly dividend from $0.1125 to $0.115 per share, for an annualized dividend of $0.46. This means the payout ratio is just around a third of the earnings, and the bank retains approximately 90 cents per share in equity on its balance sheet. This strengthens the capital ratios and boosts the potential to expand the balance sheet.

Looking at the asset side of Colony’s balance sheet, we see the bank has just over $231M in cash and about $366M in securities available for sale. The book value of the portfolio of securities held to maturity is $430M, but the fair value at the end of December was just $383M, which indeed indicates there is a $47M unrealized loss on that specific portion of the portfolio.

That being said, Colony Bankcorp runs a very liquid balance sheet with in excess of $1B of its assets invested in cash and securities.

The loan book: low LTV ratios in the CRE segment

The total size of the loan book at the end of 2024 was $1.82B, net of almost $19M in loan loss provisions. As the image below shows, the vast majority of the loan book is invested in real estate related loans, with commercial real estate playing an important role.

One element I like about Colony Bankcorp is its transparency on the loan book and its CRE exposure. The bank breaks down its loan book as below.

A high exposure to commercial real estate doesn’t have to be a negative element, as long as the vast majority of the loans are performing. As of the end of last year, approximately 99% of the loan book was indeed current,

As shown above, about $500M of the loan book consists of non-owner occupied CRE, and as you can see below, the average LTV ratio in that segment is less than 50%. This means we can consider that segment of the loan book to be pretty safe and only the high-LTV portion of the loan book will be at risk.

I will keep an eye on the loan book evolution, but I’m not too worried. The total amount of non-performing assets on the balance sheet was just $11.3M at the end of last year, which represents a $1.2M decrease compared to the preceding year.

Investment thesis

The first half of 2025 may not be too exciting for Colony Bankcorp as the bank is guiding for a stronger loan growth in the second half of the year, but the bank could be in for a strong performance as the net interest margin has started to increase again.

The consensus estimates call for an EPS of $1.65 this year and while that may be a bit too optimistic, I am confident Colony Bankcorp’s results this year will be better than last year’s results. Even if one would assume the EPS to come in at $1.50 (which is about halfway between the 2024 result and the 2025 consensus estimate), the stock isn’t overly expensive at 11 times earnings.

At the end of 2024, the book value per share was $15.91 while the tangible book value per share was $12.95. Keep in mind the TBV still uses the book value of the securities held to maturity, and if you’d incorporate the $47M difference in fair value versus book value, the TBV per share would come in at around $10.25-10.30 per share. But as the HTM portfolio matures (and as/when interest rates come down, the valuation gap should decrease as well).

Given Colony’s strong earnings performance and dividend policy, I expect the TBV to come in close to $15 by the end of next year (excluding the difference between the book value and the fair value of the securities HTM portfolio). The combination of robust earnings and an attractive P/TBV multiple makes Colony Bankcorp an interesting vehicle to gain exposure to the Georgian economy. I currently have no exposure to CBAN, but I will keep an eye on this regional bank.

data.title: Sprott: Impressive Q1 AUM Growth, Upward Revisions Of Estimates Look Likely
data.description:
Overview

Sprott Inc. (NYSE:SII)(TSX:SII:CA) is a North American investment company that focuses on precious metals and critical materials. The company is listed in the U.S. and Canada, the reporting currency is U.S. dollars. I have covered Sprott frequently going back to 2018 here on Seeking Alpha and my prior articles can be found here.

The chart below is from the Q4 2024 result, where we can see that about half of assets under management ("AUM") is in gold products, 1/4 is in silver, and the remaining portion is in critical materials, where most of that is uranium related products. The exchange listed products ("ELP") segment now accounts for over 80% of both AUM and earnings, which is why I like to focus on that segment, where recent data can be collected from the Sprott homepage.

The stock price performance of Sprott has been relatively muted in 2025, it is up 7%, but the stock has lagged gold, silver, and the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX).

That is possibly because uranium and uranium related equities have been weaker this year, as the sentiment has been terrible, and uranium equities are often correlated to the overall stock market. So, the broad market weakness has no doubt impacted uranium equities this year and possibly also Sprott.

Q4 2024 & 2024 Results

In late February, Sprott released its Q4 2024 and 2024 full year results. AUM decreased by 6% quarter-over-quarter, but that was due to a sharp decline in December in most of the segments, which will have recovered since then.

The average AUM was higher than quarter end total AUM in Q4, which is why adjusted base EBITDA had an 8% increase in Q4 2024 compared to Q3 2024. Net Income did decrease by 8% QoQ, but that was also due to the investment losses. If we adjust net income for investment gains and losses, net income increased by as much as 32% QoQ.

Another thing I have been critical about in the past is that the AUM growth has not translated to a growth in cash flow. However, in 2024, we did see a noticeable increase in operating cash flow. The company reported $69M in operating cash flow during 2024.

The strong cash flow and some investment sales has allowed Sprott to complete repaying its debt and had a cash position of $47M at the end of Q4 2024. Sprott pays a quarterly dividend of $0.30, which equates to a dividend yield of 2.7% using the latest share price.

Exchange Listed Products Segment

The exchange listed products can be tracked online, and the largest segment is off to a strong start for Sprott in 2025. The company earlier this year launched two new ETFs; the Sprott Silver Miners & Physical Silver ETF (SLVR) & the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF (GBUG).

The AUM starting point was about $2M in each ETF, but they have in just a couple of months seen healthy inflows and now have $64M in a combined AUM. In relation to the total AUM of this segment, the impact is small, but Sprott has been quite good at both launching new products or acquiring products to grow the segment over the last few years.

At the end of last year, the ELP segment had $26.1B in AUM, which has during Q1 2025 grown to $29.3B. That is a very healthy 12.4% growth rate. The 4 large trusts account for more than 90% of AUM in the segment, where the Sprott Physical Gold Trust (PHYS) now has more than $10B in AUM, which is pretty impressive when we consider that total AUM for Sprott was below $10B 5 years ago.

The Sprott Physical Uranium Trust (OTCPK:SRUUF) has in 2025 declined due to the poor performance for uranium, but the 3 large precious metals trusts have grown substantially, primarily due to the strong performance.

It is also very encouraging to see excellent inflows for the Sprott Physical Gold Trust and some more minor inflows into the Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV). The combined inflows in Q1 2025 is estimated to $576M, which is about half the inflows seen all last year in the segment. So, there is little to dislike about the trend for this segment.

Valuation & Conclusion

Using the latest share price and financials as of Q4 2024, Sprott is today trading with an EV to 2025 Earnings of 22.5 and that decreases to 19.7 and 17.8 in 2026 and 2027.

However, I would argue the estimates look very conservative. Sprott reported a net income of $1.94 in 2024, which includes minimal co-investment income and gains, but the EPS estimate for 2025 is only 1.91. Given what we have seen so far in Q1 2025 in the ELP segment, I expect the estimates to increase throughout the year. The 2027 EPS estimate of $2.42 is probably more reflective of what Sprott can deliver in 2025 already.

The valuation has come down some for Sprott lately, with an EV to my estimated Earnings somewhere around 18 and a dividend yield of 2.7%. However, my concern about excessive compensation remains, which has increased a lot more than operating cash flow or shareholder distributions over the years.

With that said, Sprott does offer mining like exposure without the same jurisdictional and operational risks and the valuation is approaching a buy for me, but I remain neutral on the stock and probably need to see a 10% share price decline to consider adding Sprott to my portfolio.

data.title: Stellus Capital: Time To Ditch This BDC (Downgrade)
data.description:
This year, business development company Stellus Capital Investment Corp. (NYSE:SCM) is possibly headed for a dividend cut as the BDC’s portfolio income has proven to be insufficient to sustain its present $0.40 per share per quarter dividend pay-out (the dividend is paid on a monthly schedule of $0.1333 per share).

Like other business development companies in the last year, Stellus Capital Investment has suffered a decrease in its credit quality which unfortunately leads to higher uncertainty about the BDC’s dividend.

As I pointed out in my piece on the business development company in December 2024, I fear that Stellus Capital Investment will follow into the footsteps of other BDCs, like Prospect Capital Corp. (PSEC) or Oaktree Specialty Lending Corp. (OCSL), and adjust its dividend lower amid insufficient net investment income.

Now that Stellus Capital Investment’s dividend pay-out ratio rose above 100% in the December-quarter and that the stock is selling for a high NII and NAV multiple compared to other business development companies, I am cutting my losses here and will move on BDCs that offer passive income investors a higher margin of safety.

Portfolio Problems Lead To High Investment Losses, High Non-Accrual Ratio And Coverage Issues

Business development companies focus on the middle market in order to provide debt capital to companies that are in need of traditional business financing. With traditional banks pulling back from higher risk lending following the GFC in 2007, BDCs like Stellus Capital Investment have stepped into the void and profited from a catalyst for portfolio and income growth.

Stellus Capital Investment is a comparatively tiny business development company with a portfolio of less than $1.0 billion and a market value below $400 million, but the BDC has benefited from growing demand for private credit and substantially grown its First Lien portfolio in the last decade.

As of December 31, 2024 Stellus Capital Investment’s portfolio value, based on fair value, sat at $954 million, up 9% YoY thanks to a steady stream of new loan originations in 2024.

As I pointed to in my prior piece on Stellus Capital Investment, the BDC’s uptick in non-accruals has held the business development company back and revelation of an even higher non-accrual ratio at the start of the month triggered a stock sell-off for SCM.

As of December 31, 2024, Stellus Capital Investment had seven loans on non-accrual status which equated to a non-accrual ratio of 5.4%. In the prior quarter, non-accruals totaled 4.7% of debt investments, so the credit profile successively deteriorated.

The increase in non-accruals led also to a decrease in SCM’s net investment income which declined from $11.9 million in 4Q23 to $9.6 million in 4Q24, reflecting a not-insignificant fall of 19% YoY. As we will see a later in this article, Stellus Capital Investment’s pay-out ratio suffered as a consequence.

On the flip side, what is positive about Stellus Capital Investment is that the business development company receives only a very low amount of payment-in-kind income which has become a problem for some BDCs like Prospect Capital as of late. Payment-in-kind income is typically non-cash and allows business development companies to mask their credit problems by deferring cash interest payments.

PIK payments have kind of skyrocketed in recent years which has led some business development companies, due to cash shortfalls, to adjust their dividend payments lower, just like Prospect Capital did recently. Though PIK income has risen broadly in the industry, it is not an issue for Stellus Capital Investment which produced only 3.3% of its total interest income from PIK sources in 2024 (3.7% in 2023).

Prospect Capital, for instance, has become heavily dependent on PIK income (12% of total interest and dividend income in 4Q24) and the decrease in payment-in-kind income was mostly behind the recent dividend cut.

Stellus Capital Investment Could Be At Risk Of A Dividend Cut

Business development companies take the money they earn from lending money to middle market companies and pay shareholders dividends. In the last couple of years, this model worked like a charm, thanks to rising interest rates which ensured steady growth in net investment income.

In the last year, however, the pressure on business development companies has grown, partially because of moderating inflation and lower interest rates. So far, the central bank has lowered short-term interest rates by 1.0% to a range of 4.25-4.50% which increased pressures on BDC’s net investment incomes.

Stellus Capital Investment earned $0.35 per share in net investment income in the December-quarter, thus under-earning its dividend pay-out by $0.05 per share. This shortfall, unfortunately, could lead to a dividend adjustment in the near-term which is why we have seen a violent downside move in the stock right after the BDC’s fourth quarter earnings presentation.

I would not be surprised to see a 15-20% dividend reduction in the next one or two quarters which would help realign the dividend with Stellus Capital Investment’s lower net investment income.

Stellus Capital reports both net investment income and core net investment income in its financial reports, the difference being that NII includes capital gains incentive fees and accruals with respect to income taxes. The difference between NII and core NII is often only just a couple of cents per quarter and does not fundamentally influence dividend pay-out metrics.

Investment Losses And NAV/NII Multiples

Stellus Capital Investment has unfortunately accumulated a high amount of investment losses in 2024, though investment losses moderated in the second half of the year.

In 2024, the BDC recognized $15.7 million in losses (net realized) which equates to $0.62 per share. To some extent investment losses (realized) were offset by a positive trend in unrealized appreciation for portfolio investments.

Because of this offset, Stellus Capital Investment’s net asset value has remained quite stable over the course of the last twelve months.

Even though Stellus Capital Investment’s dividend pay-out ratio rose above 100% in the fourth quarter, making a dividend cut possible and probable, the BDC is still one of the highest-valued business development companies in the peer group, both based on net asset value premium as well as based on NII multiple.

At the time of writing, the BDC is selling for a 6% premium to its last reported net asset value of $13.46 per share which is approximately 8% above the peer average of 0.98x NAV. Similarly, SCM is selling for the highest NII multiple, based on 4Q24 run-rate NII: It is selling at 21% premium to the BDC peer group.

In my view, Stellus Capital Investment does not deserve to a premium to either NAV or at a premium to the average P/NII ratio.

Why I Might Be Wrong, Upside and Downside Risks

Business development companies have a variety of tools in their toolbox in order to avoid dividend cuts such as restructuring or modifying loans where the borrower is not making timely payments, selling them at a loss and reinvesting proceeds into higher quality First Liens. Meaning, in the best case scenario, Stellus Capital Investment might avoid a dividend cut entirely.

On the flip side, inflation moderated down to 2.8% in February which increases pressure on the central bank to lower short-term interest rates which of course would also be a headwind for Stellus Capital Investment’s 95% floating-rate investment portfolio.

My Conclusion

Stellus Capital Investment’s dividend could unfortunately be headed for the chopping block in 2025 and I think that, taking into account the high risks of a dividend cut, the net asset value premium as well as NII premium to the peer average are not justifiable. Stellus Capital Investment’s non-accrual position (above 5.0% of fair value) is not acceptable and will probably yield a dividend cut in the near-term.

I presently perceive the BDC’s investment proposition to be highly unappealing, given the risks discussed here, and would recommend passive income investors to stay away from Stellus Capital Investment. Sell.

data.title: Betting On Gold? Consider A Currency-Hedged Vehicle Like CGL:CA
data.description:
In late-January, with Gold trading at about at about USD $2,760 per ounce (June 2025 Futures), I wrote my first article on bullion, stating, "Erosion Of U.S. Confidence And 3 Other Factors May Drive Gold To New Highs". With gold having risen about 14% in the past ~10 weeks, the position has performed well. Of course, I don't think it was rocket science, and many pundits had long been calling for the yellow metal to break above $3,000.

My article carried all the regular suspects as tickers, including the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:GLD), the iShares Gold Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:IAU) and smaller ETFs like the Sprott Physical Gold Trust (NYSEARCA:PHYS) (TSX:PHYS:CA).

However, I think I overlooked one of the best options, especially for Canadians, which is the iShares Gold Bullion ETF {CAD-Hedged} (TSX:CGL:CA). This security may outperform alongside the catalysts I've been on the lookout for. CGL.CA also has applicability to risk-tolerant U.S. Investors.

First though, let's take an updated look at my thesis.

My Catalysts For Gold To Rise

In January, I cited 5 catalysts that could lead gold to record highs. Let's take a look at each one of them here.

1) A loss of confidence in the Trump Administration's trade tactics, and acceptance that tariffs will damage the world's strongest economy.

I think I nailed the first part. I believe that concern about the current U.S. governments' approach not just towards trade, but geopolitics, has led to investor unease. That unease has led to, and will likely continue to, gold seeming like a nice warm blanket.

In terms of acceptance that trade policy will materially damage the U.S. economy, I don't think that's the base case yet. I think materially worse economic data is upcoming and will add further urgency in the U.S. to consider an alternative path.

2) A Breakdown in the U.S. Dollar.

We've definitely seen weakness in the U.S. Dollar in recent months, but I don't think it's appropriate to call it a breakdown. Let's have a look at how the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) has performed during the first quarter.

While I'd describe the decline in the DXY as modest, it's worth noting that it has broken through key moving averages, including the 200-day MA. I'm not a technician at heart, but it never hurts to complement one's analysis with technical indicators. I wouldn't be surprised if the DXY challenges its current 200-day at 104.90 in the near term. Failure to break through could result in further U.S. Dollar weakness.

For the uninitiated, the price of Gold is quoted primarily in U.S. Dollars, so a weaker USD promotes a higher Gold quote, and vice versa.

3) Renewed inflation concerns.

We did see a slight uptick in February Core PCE, but it would be premature to conclude that inflation is substantially on the rise. Due to the newness of the Trump Administration's economic policies, I think it will take a couple more months to understand the impacts on inflation.

4) A realization of Irrational Exuberance in cryptocurrency markets

Same here. I don't think this has played out to any meaningful extent. My belief is that if participants lose confidence in cryptocurrencies, they may rush into Gold as an alternative. Said another way, I don't expect capital withdrawn from cryptocurrency to find its way into U.S. Treasuries or the like.

As evident from the above chart, cryptocurrencies have definitely seen a pullback, but not to a degree that will shake the confidence of those who have been long participants.

5) An acceleration of Gold above record highs

This catalyst is circular; higher gold prices will beget higher gold prices. I think we've seen this materialize. I think bullion's break above $3,000 per ounce has lent additional credibility to the story.

Additional Upside Potential & The Appeal of CGL:CA

As evaluated above, we've been in an environment that has been constructive for the price of Gold, although I think stronger catalysts can still emerge. If the U.S. Dollar Index fails to break back above its 200-day moving average, we could see additional USD weakness that could boost gold. Additionally, should more economic data emerge that indicates that the Trump Administration's trade policies are driving inflation (which I expect it to show), that could also drive the price of gold higher.

This doesn't mean that it's smooth sailing upward, and that there aren't any risks. There are always risks of short-term profit taking, and the Gold market in particular often sees down days that go against trend. Gold investors should be prepared for that. The biggest risk, in my opinion, is an abandonment of Donald Trump's policy initiatives. While he seems steadfastly committed to tariffs for the long term, declining public opinion could result in a sudden U-turn. Normalization of U.S. policy (towards where it had stood historically in modern history) could result in a reallocation from Gold back into equities, bonds, real estate, and other assets.

The iShares Gold Bullion ETF (CAD-Hedged)

Not covered in my prior article (and rarely covered at all) are currency-hedged gold ETFs like CGL.CA.

International investors who embrace my two primary catalysts for higher gold prices (loss of U.S. Government confidence, decline in USD) could find themselves with an ugly offset, even if the thesis plays out. That's because most popular gold vehicles are denominated in U.S. Dollars, such as the GLD ETF.

In the event of a gold spike following a material pullback in the U.S. Dollar, foreign investors would find themselves no better off if the U.S. Dollar decline was equal or larger than the gold price increase. For example, if the gold price (in USD) rose another 15%, but the U.S. Dollar fell 15% against your local currency, you've gained nothing.

I know there are a lot of Canadian investors on Seeking Alpha. The iShares Gold Bullion ETF (CAD-Hedged) may be a more ideal vehicle for them to gain exposure to the price of gold. This security has, expectedly, underperformed as the USD/CAD exchange rate (USD:CAD) has risen, making it appear like an unideal choice for your portfolio.

However, with my gold thesis anchored on United States weakness, CGL.TO could easily outperform going forward. That's because this ETF hedges its currency risk, giving investors exposure to the nominal movement of gold without any currency conversion adjustments.

Furthermore, CGL.TO only carries a management expense ratio of 0.56%, which remains in the same ballpark as GLD and PHYS:CA (also Canadian, but unhedged) which has expense ratios of 0.40% and 0.41% respectively. For completeness, I should mention that the iShares Gold Trust ETF has a stated expense ratio of 0.25%.

All-in-all though, for investors who aren't prepared to conduct their own currency hedging, the fee on CGL.TO is reasonable.

While I'm directing this advice primarily to Canadian investors, American investors can also take advantage if they believe that gold will rise as a result of a declining U.S. Dollar. All you would have to do is convert some of your USD to CAD now, and purchase the CGL.TO currency hedged ETF. Should the thesis pan out, not only would you (an American investor) see the value of CGL.TO rise, but any profits would be boosted by gains made when you convert the Canadian Dollars back to USD.

Conclusion

While the price of gold has broken $3,000 and sustained its trend higher, I wouldn't at all suggest that my catalysts for higher gold prices have fully played out. There could be near-term triggers for further gains, but of course also risks of a reversal especially if the current U.S. Government policies are backtracked upon.

Investors who believe that further gains in the price of gold will be accompanied by a falling U.S. Dollar can consider a currency-hedged vehicle like CGL.TO. For Canadians, any gains in the USD price of gold risk being mitigated by currency losses is the USD declines in value.

-----

WARNING: iShares Canada also manages an unhedged gold ETF with nearly the same ticker: The iShares Gold Bullion ETF [non-hedged] (CGL.C:CA). Whatever your specific objectives, do make sure you purchase the correct ETF.

data.title: TCW Relative Value Large Cap Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Environment

The U.S. economy continues to show forward momentum. Q3 GDP was revised up to +3.1% due to higher consumer spending and service exports than previously reported. Retail sales (ex. autos) for this year’s shopping season (November 1 through December 24) came at 3.8% y/y per Mastercard SpendingPulse. The ISM PMI rose nearly two points in November from the prior month albeit still below 50. November payrolls number rebounded from October’s hurricane battered reading. The unemployment rate ticked up a notch to 4.2% (still near historical lows) while initial jobless claims remain relatively low. Inflation is still above the Federal Reserve’s (Fed) 2% target. November CPI came in at 2.7% y/y. Much of the November increase was due to stubborn shelter costs. November’s wholesale price index PPI rose 3.0% y/y a bit higher. The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge (Core CPE) for November cooled to 2.8%, its slowest pace since May. Per UBS, third quarter company earnings came in nearly twice expectations, up 9% y/y including buybacks, led in large part to mega IT and Communication Services followed by Health Care, Financials, and Utilities.

The Fed lowered rates by 25 bps at its December meeting but forecasted fewer cuts (two versus four) in 2025 due to inflation concerns. Chair Powell’s hawkish tone triggered a sharp sell-off (and spike in the VIX volatility index; 74%, the second highest single day surge ever) though stocks recouped some of the loss by month end. Mr. Powell acknowledged that inflation has improved but expressed disappointment over the “sideways” movement in recent months with shelter costs not improving as swiftly as hoped. He said, “I think we’re in a good place, but I think from here it’s a new phase and we’re going to be cautious about further cuts.”

Other central banks also cut rates in December. The Bank of Canada cut by 50 bps. In a press release, Governor Macklem said inflation and economic growth no longer require active restraint. Its economy grew by just 1% in 3Q and 4Q looks weaker than projected, and with President-elect Trump threatening a 25% tariff that is a “major source of new uncertainty.” The Swiss National Bank cut by the same magnitude amid depressed inflation and strength in its franc. There is chatter that the central bank may flirt with negative interest rates “if necessary.” The European Central Bank cut by 25 bps though there was some discussion to go with 50 bps and noted that disinflation is “well on track.” President Lagarde signaled that path of rate cuts is downward. The Reserve Bank of Australia made dovish comments but did not cut, the last of the G7 central banks except the Bank of Japan (BoJ) yet to ease.

In order to maintain its 5% GDP goals, the People’s Bank of China has put forth a broad stimulative package to combat slowing growth and waning investor confidence. In November, China announced a $1.4 trillion program (over five years) allowing local governments to refinance debt. More recently, authorities agreed to issue 3 trillion yuan ($411 billion) worth of special treasury bonds in the new year. Recent inflation data in Japan increases the likelihood of a BoJ rate hike(s). The increase was largely due to a winding down of government utility subsidies, something which has prompted Prime Minister Ishiba to reinstate from January to March as part of his economic stimulus package.

Sector Weightings 1

For the fourth quarter, the best performing sectors in the Russell 1000 Value were financials (+7.1%), communication services (+2.4%), and information technology (-0.4%). The bottom performers were materials (-11.5%), health care (-10.4%), and real estate (-6.8%). The portfolio sector’s weights contributed favorably led by the overweight in financials and underweights in health care and materials.

Security Selection 1

Stock selection in the quarter contributed noticeably. The portfolio’s top ten average-weighted names (over the course of the quarter) returned 6.4%, on average, better than the portfolio and benchmark index led by solid gains from Broadcom (AVGO; 4.04%**) and Morgan Stanley (MS; 2.56%**). The portfolio’s information technology and financials stock selection provided the preponderance of the relative outperformance. In IT, the portfolio’s stocks returned 7.7% far ahead of their peers’ -0.4% loss led largely by Broadcom with added support from Salesforce (CRM; 1.74%**) and Flex (FLEX; 1.92%**). In financials, the portfolio’s names’ rose 11.7% versus the group gain of 7.1% highlighted by Apollo Global Management (APO; 1.96%**), Morgan Stanley, and Fiserv (FI; 2.71%**). One additional notable outperformer included McKesson (MCK; 3.04%**).

On the bottom end, the most noticeable laggard was homebuilder Lennar (LEN; 2.75%**). The stock fell after the company posted weaker than expected results and lowered guidance with uncertainty around mortgage rates. Lennar’s underperformance raised a fundamental review flag and within five business days, the position was added to slightly with an eye toward the upcoming spring home buying season. GE Aerospace (GE; 2.92%**) was largely responsible for the underperformance in industrials where the portfolio’s stocks fell -5.8% versus the group return of -2.1%. The portfolio’s consumer staples names declined -13.9% versus their peers’ loss of -2.7% mostly due to Target (TGT; 0.89%**). Its stock fell sharply after reporting both revenues and earnings below analysts’ expectations. On the earnings call, the company’s CEO cited a more discerning consumer whose budgets “remain stretched” and who are “focusing on deals” and “stocking up when they fine them.” Target was reduced on its fundamental review flag. AES’s (AES; 0.64%**) sharp decline was mostly to blame for the decline in utilities though its relatively small weighting softened the blow. Its stock fell after reporting mixed results with earnings beating but revenues falling short. Additionally, AES, along with utilities in general, has come under pressure with more attractive income opportunities with the recent spike in bond interest rates. With the recent strengthening in the U.S. dollar, materials stocks have felt the brunt and portfolio holding Freeport-McMoRan (FCX; 1.14%**) was not immune leading to underperformance relative to their peers. Other notable detractors included health care names Elevance Health (ELV; 0.63%**) and Amgen (AMGN; 1.81%**).

Representative Buys (Ticker, Sector)*

Procter & Gamble (PG; Consumer Staples) (0.51%**)

MSCI ESG Score 2: Overall AA

Procter & Gamble (PG), headquartered in Cincinnati OH, is one of the world’s largest manufacturers of branded consumer packaged goods. PG operates within five segments: 1) Fabric and Home Care, 2) Baby, Feminine and Family Care, 3) Beauty, 4) Grooming, and 5) Healthcare. The company’s leading brands include Tide, Cascade, Crest, Gillette, Vicks, Head & Shoulders, Pantene, Olay, Charmin, Bounty, just to name a few. At initiation, PG had a $400 billion market capitalization and met one (and statistically the most correlative) of five valuation factors, dividend yield of 2.4%. The catalyst is cost cutting and restructuring. The company has a long-term track record of delivering substantial productivity savings with operating margin expansion of nearly 300 bps over the last five years. PG’s management team expects impressive productivity gains of at least $2 billion in FY25 and has a strong pipeline of opportunities over the next few years which should drive strong margin expansion.

UnitedHealthcare Group Inc. (UNH: Health Care) (0.66%**)

MSCI ESG Score: Overall A

UnitedHealthcare Group, headquartered in Minnetonka, MN, is the largest provider of healthcare insurance plans and other related healthcare services in the United States. UNH offers a broad spectrum of network-based managed care health benefit plans to the Medicare, Medicaid, Healthcare Exchanges, government, employee, individual markets. In addition to medical and pharmacy benefits services, the company operates through the following segments: UnitedHealthcare, Optum Rx, Optum Health and Optum Insight. UnitedHealthcare also offer healthcare insurance products for Medicare, Medicaid, Healthcare Exchanges, as well as for commercial employers and individuals. At initiation, UNH had

$568 billion market capitalization and met three of the five valuation factors (price-to-sales, price-to-earnings and dividend yield). The catalysts are new management and new markets/new products. In February 2021, Andrew Witty was appointed as CEO and Director. The company has expanded its home health and behavioral offerings and other medical services. The company is also undertaking a strong program to enhance efficiency and business optimization through technology applied to various aspects of its business from medical utilization to real estate optimization and integrate AI capability across all its business aspects. The successful implementation of these catalysts should lead to improved operating margins, cash flow generation, and increased earnings.

Representative Sells (Ticker Sector)*†

There were no complete sells this quarter.

Market Outlook

Stock prices should continue to follow earnings growth. Corporate earnings are expected to increase in Q4 from their Q3 pace to ~12% with banks poised to be the biggest contributor based on easier compares lapping FDIC special assessments; semiconductors and pharmaceuticals are expected to be #2 and #3. Expectations are for low- to mid-teens earnings growth in 2025. We expect the broad U.S. markets to rise 12-15% in 2025, more should fiscal actions continue to flow to consumers and businesses. The Magnificent 7 Q3 reports demonstrated their slowest collective expansion in six quarters. What’s more, the earnings gap between Big Tech and the rest of the market is expected to continue to narrow into 2025 which should turn the tide in favor of value factors.

Slower growth but no hard landing should be supportive for other companies not already buoyed by AI infrastructure. Additional points for value outperformance in 2025 include lower corporate taxes which should favor currently higher tax paying U.S. domestic sectors such as Financials and Health Care. Also, historically, changes in U.S. presidential regimes change the underlying market dynamics. One example is value outperforming growth in 2016 (17.3% vs. 7.1%) followed by growth outperforming value in 2017 (30.2% v. 13.7%) as represented by the Russell 1000 Value and Growth Indices. We expect regulatory relief will be a positive for Financials, Industrials, and small- to medium-size businesses. There is increased anxiety and potential volatility this year with Trump’s pro-tariff stance and, with it, the possibility for upward pressure on inflation. As part of his first executive orders, Trump has stated that he will impose additional 10% tariffs on Chinese goods and 25% on Mexico and Canada citing “crime and drugs.”

The performance headwind facing the portfolio’s underweights in expensive megacaps could become a tailwind in 2025. We believe the U.S. is a global haven for all asset classes particularly on the back of a strong U.S dollar. Geopolitical risks abound and the Eurozone’s two largest economies, France and Germany, face internal strife. Canada as well is facing slow growth and a government regime change. We are busy and actively working to make the best portfolio decisions possible with the information available and will adjust should the “facts” change. We are constructive about the future but maintain strong risk controls for unexpected turbulent events.

Diane E. Jaffee, CFA, Group Managing Director

Matthew J. Spahn, Managing Director
